front: Define Kernel (Null Space) of a Linear Transformation T: V → W
back: The kernel of T, denoted ker(T) or null(T), is the set of all vectors v in V such that T(v) = 0. Formally: ker(T) = {v ∈ V | T(v) = 0_w}. It is a subspace of V.

front: Define Image (Range) of a Linear Transformation T: V → W
back: The image of T, denoted im(T) or range(T), is the set of all vectors w in W such that w = T(v) for some v in V. Formally: im(T) = {w ∈ W | w = T(v) for some v ∈ V}. It is a subspace of W.
For a linear transformation T: V → W where dim(V) = n, we have: rank(T) + nullity(T) = n, where rank(T) = dim(im(T)) and nullity(T) = dim(ker(T)).

front: When is a Linear Transformation T one-to-one (injective)?
back: T is one-to-one if and only if ker(T) = {0}. This means the only vector that maps to zero is the zero vector itself. Equivalently, T(u) = T(v) implies u = v.

front: When is a Linear Transformation T onto (surjective)?
back: T is onto (surjective) if and only if im(T) = W, meaning every vector in the codomain W has a preimage in V. The image spans the entire codomain.

front: What is the relationship between kernel and one-to-one property?
back: A linear transformation T: V → W is one-to-one if and only if ker(T) = {0}. If any non-zero vector maps to zero, then distinct vectors could map to the same output, violating injectivity.

front: Define Rank and Nullity of a Linear Transformation
back: Rank(T) = dim(im(T)) = dimension of the image subspace. Nullity(T) = dim(ker(T)) = dimension of the kernel subspace. Both are non-negative integers.

front: How do you find the kernel of a matrix transformation T(x) = Ax?
back: Solve the homogeneous system Ax = 0. The kernel is the solution set to this system, which forms a subspace. Use Gaussian elimination to find the general solution.

front: How do you find the image of a matrix transformation T(x) = Ax?
back: The image is spanned by the column vectors of matrix A. Find the column space by determining which columns are linearly independent (through row reduction).

front: If T: R^n → R^m is a linear transformation with matrix A, what is dim(ker(T))?
back: The nullity equals n - rank(A), where rank(A) is the rank of matrix A. This follows from the Rank-Nullity Theorem: nullity = n - rank.

front: What is the kernel of the zero transformation T: V → W where T(v) = 0 for all v?
back: The kernel is the entire domain V, since T(v) = 0 for every v ∈ V. The image is {0} (only the zero vector in W).

front: What is the image of the identity transformation I: V → V where I(v) = v?
back: The image is the entire codomain V (im(I) = V). The kernel is {0} since only I(0) = 0.

front: If T: V → W is onto and dim(V) = dim(W), what can we say about T?
back: If T is onto and dim(V) = dim(W), then T is also one-to-one. Conversely, if T is one-to-one and dimensions are equal, T is onto. This is an isomorphism.

front: How is the rank of a matrix related to the image of the transformation?
back: The rank of matrix A equals the dimension of the column space (image) of the transformation T(x) = Ax. rank(A) = dim(im(T)).
